claim: Use the Fisherman's Prompt to learn any technical topic with LLMs: structure it as 'I'm a [level] [profession] wanting to learn [topic] for [objective]'
actionable: Adopt the Fisherman's Prompt template with SUB_TOPICS and RULES sections to create personalized mini-courses from LLMs

claim: Use concrete examples and emojis in learning prompts; ask for clarification at end of chapters; explain real-world use cases for every concept
actionable: When using LLMs to learn, enforce concrete examples over abstractions, use visuals, and ask questions actively

claim: AI tutors remove judgment pressure that inhibits learning in group settings, making them effective for knowledge acquisition
actionable: Use AI tutors for learning topics where you feel behind or uncertain; there's no social penalty for asking basic questions

claim: The Fisherman's Prompt is versatile: you can learn Pytest, Rust, HTMX, or anything within LLM knowledge cutoff by changing [topic] and SUB_TOPICS
actionable: Use the Fisherman's Prompt template for any new technical skill; it scales from programming languages to frameworks to testing tools

claim: Software engineering is about constant learning; AI tutors accelerate learning speed, making continuous skill development feasible
actionable: Adopt continuous learning as a practice; use AI tutors to stay current as tech stacks and tools evolve weekly

claim: Ask follow-up questions during learning instead of passively consuming; the chat interface enables active, iterative learning
actionable: When learning with LLMs, pause after each chapter and ask clarifying questions about concepts that seem unclear
